content:
Why No-Instrumentation Tracing Matters

Distributed tracing is supposed to require SDK instrumentation. You add the OpenTelemetry library to each service, instrument spans manually, propagate trace context through HTTP headers, and export to your backend. For a homogeneous codebase where you control every service and every deployment, this works. For a heterogeneous environment with compiled binaries you cannot modify — legacy C daemons, third-party services, statically linked binaries — it does not.

eBPF kprobes operate below the language layer, at the syscall boundary. Every network operation a process makes — sending a request, receiving a response, accepting a connection — goes through a syscall. connect, sendto, recvfrom, accept. eBPF programs can attach to the entry and exit points of any syscall and record the PID, TID, syscall number, and a kernel timestamp without modifying the process in any way.

How Spans Are Generated From Syscalls

A span is the interval between a syscall's entry and exit. The eBPF program records an entry event on sys_enter_* and an exit event on sys_exit_*. The Rust userspace daemon matches entry to exit by PID and TID within a 50ms correlation window, computes the duration from kernel monotonic timestamps, and constructs an OpenTelemetry span. The span has a start time, end time, process identifier, and syscall type as the operation name.

The correlation window tradeoff

The 50ms window is the time the daemon waits for a matching exit event after seeing an entry. Too short and slow syscalls (large file reads, long-polling HTTP calls) produce incomplete spans. Too long and the in-flight correlation table grows without bound under high concurrency, increasing memory pressure. 50ms was derived from the p99 syscall duration for the workloads under observation. A different workload profile — one with frequent long-polling calls — would require re-tuning to 200ms or more, with a corresponding increase in table memory usage.

Bounded MPSC Back-Pressure

The ring buffer consumer and the OTLP exporter run in separate threads connected by a bounded MPSC channel with a capacity of 8,192 spans. When the exporter falls behind — network latency to Jaeger, serialization overhead, Jaeger unavailability — the channel fills and the consumer drops the oldest span. This is a deliberate design choice: exporter stalls must not propagate back to the kernel ring buffer. A stalled kernel-side producer affects the applications being observed, which defeats the purpose.

Drop-oldest vs drop-newest

Drop-oldest eviction means recent spans are preserved at the cost of older ones. For distributed tracing, recent spans are more operationally useful — you care most about what the system is doing right now, not what it was doing 30 seconds ago when the exporter first fell behind. Drop-newest (blocking the producer) is categorically wrong for this use case: it would apply backpressure up to the kernel ring buffer, causing the applications under observation to experience increased syscall latency.

Clock Skew: The Silent Data Corruption

Kernel BPF programs use bpf_ktime_get_ns() which returns monotonic nanoseconds since boot — not wall clock time. The userspace daemon converts to wall clock by sampling the offset between CLOCK_MONOTONIC and CLOCK_REALTIME at startup. This offset drifts over time due to NTP adjustments and clock frequency corrections.

If the offset changes by more than 1ms between samples, a clock_skew_warning_total counter is incremented and an Alertmanager rule fires. Undetected clock skew produces spans with negative durations in Jaeger — durations calculated as exit_time - entry_time where the times were converted using different offsets. Jaeger silently discards negative-duration spans. This makes it appear that tracing is working while producing no data for affected requests. It is the worst kind of failure: silent and misleading.

Trace Context Propagation Without SDK

For HTTP services, W3C Trace Context headers (traceparent, tracestate) are extracted from sendto and recvfrom syscall buffers by inspecting the first 512 bytes of the payload for HTTP header patterns. When a traceparent header is found in an outgoing request, the span generated from that sendto is tagged with the parent trace ID. When found in an incoming request, the accept span is linked as a child.

This allows the pipeline to correlate spans across service boundaries without SDK instrumentation in either service. A span from Service A's connect links to Service B's accept if the HTTP request carries a traceparent header from any source — including a properly instrumented upstream service that already has SDK tracing. The eBPF pipeline integrates with existing instrumented services without requiring changes to either.

01

The most valuable traces were the ones that revealed unexpected syscall patterns — a service making 40,000 stat() calls per request due to a misconfigured file watcher. This is invisible to application-level tracing because the application code never calls a traced function. eBPF at the syscall layer surfaces problems genuinely undetectable from inside the application.

02

OTLP/gRPC requires TLS in production Jaeger deployments. Reusing the Vault PKI backend already present for Kubernetes node certificates — rather than introducing a new CA — simplified the trust model significantly. Every additional CA in a system is an operational burden that compounds over time.

03

Clock skew detection is not optional. In a fleet where NTP is actively managing clock frequency, the monotonic-to-wall-clock offset changes continuously. Without periodic re-sampling and alerting, you will eventually produce negative-duration spans that disappear silently from your trace backend.

04

The 512-byte HTTP header inspection window is a heuristic. HTTP/2 uses HPACK header compression — traceparent headers in HTTP/2 traffic are not visible as plaintext in the syscall buffer. This pipeline provides full context propagation for HTTP/1.1 and partial propagation for HTTP/2. HTTP/3 (QUIC) is not supported — QUIC operates in userspace and does not use the standard TCP syscall path.
